## Summary
Attention Schema Theory (AST) proposes that consciousness is the brain's internal model of its own attentional processes, analogous to the body schema modeling the body. The brain constructs a simplified, schematic representation of attention — the "attention schema" — and this model is what we experience as subjective awareness.

## Evidence
AST draws support from the temporo-parietal junction's dual role in attention control and social cognition (attributing awareness to self and others). Patients with TPJ damage show both attentional deficits and impaired theory of mind. Computational models demonstrate that an attention schema improves attentional control in artificial systems.

## Significance
AST offers a deflationary account of consciousness — subjective experience is a useful model, not a mysterious extra. It naturally explains why we attribute consciousness to others (social cognition uses the same schema) and makes testable predictions about the relationship between attention and awareness.
